1:
"And after Abimelech there arose to defend Israel Tola the son of Puah, the son of Dodo, a man of Issachar; and he dwelt in Shamir in mount Ephraim." The very brief verse notes Tola of Issachar arising to defend Israel after Abimelech. The grammatical voices note the judge. Keil and Delitzsch and Gill note that "after Abimelech there arose to defend Israel Tola the son of Puah... a man of Issachar; and he dwelt in Shamir in mount Ephraim" — Tola of Issachar rose to save Israel after Abimelech's death; the and after Abimelech there arose to defend Israel Tola the son of Puah, the son of Dodo, a man of Issachar (Tola arose); the and he dwelt in Shamir in mount Ephraim (his seat); the figure (the quiet deliverer raised up after the usurper, God's mercy renewed; the truth that God raised Tola to defend Israel after the ruin of Abimelech, Judges 2:16; the figure of God's mercy in raising deliverers, the peaceful judge after the tyrant; the rise of Tola; the judge raised, vv.16). The verse holds together the rise of Tola.

6:
"And the children of Israel did evil again in the sight of the LORD, and served Baalim, and Ashtaroth, and the gods of Syria, and the gods of Zidon, and the gods of Moab, and the gods of the children of Ammon, and the gods of the Philistines, and forsook the LORD, and served not him." The witnesses note Israel's deep apostasy, serving the gods of all the surrounding nations and forsaking the LORD — the crux of the heart that, having left the one true God, plunges into every idolatry without restraint. On "served Baalim, and Ashtaroth, and the gods of Syria... and forsook the LORD, and served not him," interpreters expound the relapse. The reformed Calvin and the dominant reading note the deep and manifold apostasy — "and the children of Israel did evil again in the sight of the LORD, and served Baalim, and Ashtaroth, and the gods of Syria, and the gods of Zidon, and the gods of Moab, and the gods of the children of Ammon, and the gods of the Philistines, and forsook the LORD, and served not him": Israel sank to a deeper idolatry than ever, serving the gods of all the nations round about; the truth that to forsake God is to be enslaved to a multitude of false masters, the soul that will not serve the one Lord becoming the servant of many lords, "they that go after another god shall multiply their sorrows," Psalm 16:4; The figure of the heart that, having left the one true God, plunges into every idolatry without restraint.

Keil and Ellicott note Israel serving the gods of seven nations, a deeper and wider idolatry than ever, the heart given over to every false god (the manifold apostasy, Jeremiah 2:13) — the idolatry (the gods of the nations).

On "and forsook the LORD, and served not him," the reformed Calvin and grammatical-historical note the forsaking. Calvin and Gill note Israel utterly forsaking the LORD and serving him not at all, the soul that leaves the one Lord enslaved to many, multiplying its sorrows (the forsaking of God, Psalm 16:4; Romans 1:28) — the figure (the bottomless descent of idolatry once the true God is forsaken); the providence (the apostasy, God selling them to the Philistines and Ammon to follow, vv.7). The verse displays Israel serving the gods of all the nations and forsaking the LORD.

The witnesses agree the substance: Israel sank to a deeper idolatry than ever, serving the gods of all the nations and forsaking the LORD (Calvin, Keil, Ellicott, Gill). The witnesses agree: the rehearsal — "Did not I deliver you from the Egyptians, and from the Amorites, from the children of Ammon, and from the Philistines?" the rehearsal (Keil, Gill); "And the LORD said unto the children of Israel, Did not I deliver you from the Egyptians, and from the Amorites," the deliverances (Psalm 106:43, Gill); "from the children of Ammon, and from the Philistines?" the many foes (Ellicott, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the rehearsal. The verse marks God: recalling. The verse displays the deliverances: many. The verse displays Israel: convicted. The verse displays the ingratitude: exposed. The rehearsal displays the God who pleads his past mercies against the ingratitude of his people. The verse displays the record: long. The verse displays the mercies: recalled. The verse displays the shame: pressed. God rehearsing his past deliverances displays the God who pleads his past mercies against ingratitude: Did not I deliver you from the Egyptians, and from the Amorites, from the children of Ammon, and from the Philistines? (Psalm 106:43) — God recalling the many times he had delivered Israel, pleading his past mercies against their apostasy and ingratitude. The verse displays God continuing the record, of the Zidonians, Amalekites, and Maonites (vv.12).
12:
"The Zidonians also, and the Amalekites, and the Maonites, did oppress you; and ye cried to me, and I delivered you out of their hand." The very brief verse notes God continuing the record of deliverances, when Israel cried he saved them. The grammatical voices note the record. Keil and Delitzsch and Gill note that "the Zidonians also, and the Amalekites, and the Maonites, did oppress you; and ye cried to me, and I delivered you out of their hand" — God recalled more oppressors from whom he had delivered Israel at their cry; the the Zidonians also, and the Amalekites, and the Maonites, did oppress you (more oppressors); the and ye cried to me, and I delivered you out of their hand (and I delivered you); the figure (the long faithfulness of God to deliver at every cry, the mercy that answered always; the truth that whenever Israel cried God delivered them, his mercy unfailing, Nehemiah 9:28; the figure of the God who has always answered the cry of his people, the faithful deliverer; the record continued; the mercy that answered, vv.11). The verse holds together the record.

On "The Zidonians also, and the Amalekites, and the Maonites, did oppress you," the witnesses note the oppressors. Gill notes the further oppressors recalled (the record, Nehemiah 9:28) — the oppressors (Zidonians, Amalekites).

On "and ye cried to me, and I delivered you out of their hand," the grammatical-historical Ellicott and grammatical-historical note the deliverance: "ye cried to me, and I delivered you out of their hand" — God delivering at every cry (the faithful mercy); the figure (the God who has always answered the cry of his people); the providence (the record, God's poignant refusal to follow, vv.13). The verse displays God continuing the record of his deliverances.

The very brief comment matches the very brief verse. The verse displays the record. The verse marks the oppressors: many. The verse displays the cry: answered. The verse displays God: delivering. The verse displays the mercy: unfailing. The record displays the God who has always answered the cry of his people. The verse displays the faithfulness: long. The verse displays the deliverance: given. The verse displays the cry: heard. God continuing the record of his deliverances displays the God who has always answered the cry of his people: the Zidonians also, and the Amalekites, and the Maonites, did oppress you; and ye cried to me, and I delivered you out of their hand (Nehemiah 9:28) — God recalling more oppressors from whom he had delivered Israel at their cry, the faithful deliverer who has always answered his people's cry. The verse displays God's poignant refusal, I will deliver you no more (vv.13).
13:
"Yet ye have forsaken me, and served other gods: wherefore I will deliver you no more." The witnesses note God's solemn refusal, that because Israel had forsaken him for other gods he would deliver them no more — the crux of the just refusal of grace abused, the holy God who will not be mocked by a repentance born only of distress. On "ye have forsaken me, and served other gods: wherefore I will deliver you no more," interpreters expound the refusal. The reformed Calvin and the dominant reading note the just refusal — "yet ye have forsaken me, and served other gods: wherefore I will deliver you no more": God, having rehearsed his mercies, declares he will deliver them no more; the truth that a repentance born only of distress, that seeks deliverance but not the Deliverer, is not yet true, and God's refusal searches whether the heart will turn to him or only from its pain, "they returned and inquired early after God... nevertheless they did flatter him," Psalm 78:34-37; the figure — the holy severity that is the servant of mercy, the refusal meant to drive the soul from its idols to the only Saviour, fulfilled in the gospel's warning that grace despised is grace endangered, "how shall we escape, if we neglect so great salvation?" Hebrews 2:3. The figure of the just refusal of grace abused, the holy God who will not be mocked by a repentance born only of distress.

On "yet ye have forsaken me, and served other gods," the grammatical-historical Keil and witnesses note the ingratitude charged. Keil and Ellicott note God charging Israel with forsaking him and serving other gods despite all his deliverances, the ingratitude that had repaid every mercy with apostasy (the grace abused, Proverbs 1:24-26) — the charge (ye have forsaken me).

On "wherefore I will deliver you no more," the reformed Calvin and grammatical-historical note the refusal. Calvin and Gill note God declaring he would deliver them no more, the holy rebuke meant to humble them and search whether their cry was true repentance, the grace abused that turns to righteous refusal, the seeming denial the servant of a deeper mercy (the refusal that searches the heart, Psalm 78:34-37; Hebrews 2:3) — the figure (the holy severity that is the servant of mercy); the providence (the refusal, Israel's true repentance to follow, vv.15-16). The verse displays God's refusal to deliver Israel any more.

The witnesses agree the substance: God, having rehearsed his mercies, declares he will deliver them no more (Calvin, Keil, Ellicott, Gill). The verse bears its weight as the crux of grace abused: I will deliver you no more, the solemn refusal meant not to abandon but to humble, to bring home their guilt and test whether their cry was true repentance or mere desire for relief, God not wearied of mercy but resolved they should feel the weight of their sin, the grace abused that turns at last to righteous refusal (Proverbs 1:24-26), the repentance born only of distress not yet true (Psalm 78:34-37), the holy severity that is the servant of mercy and drives the soul from its idols to the Saviour (Hebrews 2:3). The verse displays the just-refusal crux. God's refusal to deliver Israel any more displays the crux: ye have forsaken me, and served other gods: wherefore I will deliver you no more (Proverbs 1:24-26; Psalm 78:34-37; Hebrews 2:3) — the just refusal of grace abused, the holy God who will not be mocked by a repentance born only of distress, the severity that is the servant of mercy, meant to drive Israel from its idols to the only Saviour. The verse displays Israel's true repentance, submitting to God's judgment and putting away their gods (vv.15-16).
14:
"Go and cry unto the gods which ye have chosen; let them deliver you in the time of your tribulation." The witnesses note God's biting irony, bidding Israel cry to the gods they had chosen and let those deliver them — the crux of the holy irony that exposes the impotence of idols and the folly of forsaking the living God. On "Go and cry unto the gods which ye have chosen; let them deliver you in the time of your tribulation," interpreters expound the irony. The reformed Calvin and the dominant reading note the exposing of idols — "Go and cry unto the gods which ye have chosen; let them deliver you in the time of your tribulation": God ironically bids them seek help from the idols they preferred to him; the truth that whatever a man puts in God's place will fail him in his extremity, the chosen idol dumb in the hour of need, "can any of the vanities of the Gentiles cause rain?" Jeremiah 14:22; the figure — the bankruptcy of every false confidence, fulfilled in the day when all who trusted other saviours find them helpless, "where are thy gods that thou hast made thee? let them arise, if they can save thee," Jeremiah 2:28. The figure of the holy irony that exposes the impotence of idols and the folly of forsaking the living God.

On "Go and cry unto the gods which ye have chosen," the grammatical-historical Keil and witnesses note the gods of their choosing. Keil and Ellicott note God bidding them cry to the gods they had chosen in his stead, the choice of idols thrown back upon them (the chosen idols, Psalm 115:5-8) — the irony (cry to your chosen gods).

On "let them deliver you in the time of your tribulation," the reformed Calvin and grammatical-historical note the impotence exposed. Calvin and Gill note God bidding the idols deliver them, exposing their utter impotence to save in the hour of need, the false confidence bankrupt, the chosen god dumb in the day of trouble (the idols that cannot save, Jeremiah 14:22; Jeremiah 2:28) — the figure (the bankruptcy of every false confidence); the providence (the irony, Israel's true repentance to follow, vv.15). The verse displays God bidding Israel cry to the gods they had chosen.

The witnesses agree the substance: God ironically bids Israel seek help from the idols they preferred to him (Calvin, Keil, Ellicott, Gill). The verse bears its weight as the crux of the holy irony: let them deliver you — the cutting irony of God, who bids the apostates turn for deliverance to the very gods they had chosen in his stead, that they might feel the folly and shame of their idolatry and find the idols powerless to save, the irony a holy rebuke meant to drive them to despair of their idols and back to God, the chosen idols utterly unable to save and exposed in the day of trouble (Psalm 115:5-8), whatever a man puts in God's place failing him in his extremity (Jeremiah 14:22), the bankruptcy of every false confidence (Jeremiah 2:28). The verse displays the holy-irony crux. God bidding Israel cry to the gods they had chosen displays the crux: Go and cry unto the gods which ye have chosen; let them deliver you in the time of your tribulation (Psalm 115:5-8; Jeremiah 14:22; Jeremiah 2:28) — the holy irony that exposes the impotence of idols and the folly of forsaking the living God, the chosen gods summoned to do what only God can do and found helpless, the irony mercy in disguise meant to shame the soul out of its idols. The verse displays Israel's true repentance, owning their sin and submitting to God (vv.15).
15:
"And the children of Israel said unto the LORD, We have sinned: do thou unto us whatsoever seemeth good unto thee; deliver us only, we pray thee, this day." The witnesses note Israel's true repentance, confessing their sin and submitting wholly to God's will while pleading only for deliverance — the crux of the genuine repentance that casts itself unreservedly upon the mercy of God. On "We have sinned: do thou unto us whatsoever seemeth good unto thee; deliver us only, we pray thee, this day," interpreters expound the submission. The reformed Calvin and the dominant reading note the unreserved submission of faith — "and the children of Israel said unto the LORD, We have sinned: do thou unto us whatsoever seemeth good unto thee; deliver us only, we pray thee, this day": Israel confesses sin, submits to whatever God sees fit, and casts itself on his mercy; the truth that the broken and submissive heart is the one God will not despise, the soul that says "do unto me as seemeth good" laying hold of mercy by its very submission, "a broken and a contrite heart, O God, thou wilt not despise," Psalm 51:17; the figure — the penitent who casts himself wholly on God, fulfilled in the prodigal who returns confessing and the publican who cries for mercy, "God be merciful to me a sinner," Luke 18:13. The figure of the genuine repentance that casts itself unreservedly upon the mercy of God.

On "and the children of Israel said unto the LORD, We have sinned: do thou unto us whatsoever seemeth good unto thee," the grammatical-historical Keil and witnesses note the submission to God's will. Keil and Ellicott note Israel confessing their sin and submitting to whatever God saw fit, the entire resignation to his righteous will, the heart bowing to his justice (the submission of faith, Micah 7:9) — the submission (whatsoever seemeth good).

On "deliver us only, we pray thee, this day," the reformed Calvin and grammatical-historical note the appeal to mercy. Calvin and Gill note Israel casting itself wholly on God's free mercy, pleading only for deliverance and not on their desert, the broken and contrite heart God will not despise, the penitent who casts himself on God alone (the appeal to mercy, Psalm 51:17; Luke 18:13) — the figure (the penitent who casts himself wholly on God); the providence (the repentance, Israel putting away their gods to follow, vv.16). The verse displays Israel's confession, submission, and appeal to mercy.

The witnesses agree the substance: Israel confesses sin, submits to whatever God sees fit, and casts itself on his mercy (Calvin, Keil, Ellicott, Gill). The verse bears its weight as the crux of true repentance: do thou unto us whatsoever seemeth good... deliver us only, an entire submission to God's will, and an appeal cast wholly on his free mercy and not their own desert, the soul that has felt God's refusal now bowing to his justice and throwing itself on his grace, true repentance submitting to God's will and appealing only to mercy (Micah 7:9), the broken and contrite heart God will not despise (Psalm 51:17), the penitent who casts himself wholly on God (Luke 18:13). The verse displays the true-repentance crux. Israel's confession, submission, and appeal to mercy display the crux: We have sinned: do thou unto us whatsoever seemeth good unto thee; deliver us only, we pray thee, this day (Micah 7:9; Psalm 51:17; Luke 18:13) — the genuine repentance that casts itself unreservedly upon the mercy of God, confessing sin, submitting to his will, and appealing only to his grace, the very door through which mercy returns. The verse displays Israel putting away their strange gods and serving the LORD (vv.16).
16:
"And they put away the strange gods from among them, and served the LORD: and his soul was grieved for the misery of Israel." The witnesses note Israel putting away their idols and serving the LORD, and the LORD's soul being grieved for the misery of Israel — the crux of the tender compassion of God, who is moved by the misery of his people and cannot long withhold his mercy from the penitent. On "they put away the strange gods from among them, and served the LORD: and his soul was grieved for the misery of Israel," interpreters expound the turning and the pity. The reformed Calvin and the dominant reading note the compassion of God — "and they put away the strange gods from among them, and served the LORD: and his soul was grieved for the misery of Israel": Israel proves its repentance by deeds, and God is moved with pity at their misery; his fatherly heart yearning over them the moment they turn; the lesson — that God is full of tender mercy toward the truly penitent, his compassions failing not, his heart turned the moment his people turn, "in all their affliction he was afflicted... in his love and in his pity he redeemed them," Isaiah 63:9; the truth that the misery of his people moves the heart of God, who afflicts not willingly and is grieved at their grief, "how shall I give thee up?... mine heart is turned within me, my repentings are kindled together," Hosea 11:8; The figure of the tender compassion of God, who is moved by the misery of his people and cannot long withhold his mercy from the penitent.

On "and they put away the strange gods from among them, and served the LORD," the grammatical-historical Keil and witnesses note the repentance proved by reformation. Keil and Ellicott note Israel putting away their idols and actually returning to God's service, the true repentance proved by forsaking the idols and doing the deeds of reformation (the repentance made real, Isaiah 63:9) — the reformation (put away the strange gods).

On "and his soul was grieved for the misery of Israel," the reformed Calvin and grammatical-historical note the compassion of God. Calvin and Gill note the LORD's soul grieved, shortened, impatient at the misery of his people, the tender compassion of God moved as a father over a suffering child, his heart turned the moment they turned, his mercy unable to endure their distress (the divine pity, Hosea 11:8; Luke 15:20) — the figure (the father running to meet the returning prodigal, moved with compassion); the providence (the compassion, the gathering against Ammon and the rise of Jephthah to follow, vv.17-18; ch.11). The verse displays Israel's reformation and the LORD's compassion for their misery.

The witnesses agree the substance: Israel proves its repentance by putting away the idols and serving the LORD, and God is moved with pity at their misery (Calvin, Keil, Ellicott, Gill). The verse bears its weight as the crux of God's compassion: his soul was grieved for the misery of Israel, his fatherly heart yearning over them the moment they turn, God full of tender mercy toward the penitent, afflicted in their affliction (Isaiah 63:9), the father running to meet the returning prodigal (Luke 15:20). The verse displays the divine-compassion crux. Israel's reformation and the LORD's compassion for their misery display the crux: they put away the strange gods from among them, and served the LORD: and his soul was grieved for the misery of Israel (Isaiah 63:9; Hosea 11:8; Luke 15:20) — the tender compassion of God. The verse displays the gathering of Ammon and Israel, and the people seeking a captain — the rise of Jephthah (vv.17-18; ch.11).
17:
"Then the children of Ammon were gathered together, and encamped in Gilead. And the children of Israel assembled themselves together, and encamped in Mizpeh." The very brief verse notes Ammon and Israel gathering for war in Gilead and Mizpeh. The grammatical voices note the muster. Keil and Delitzsch and Gill note that "the children of Ammon were gathered together, and encamped in Gilead. And the children of Israel assembled themselves together, and encamped in Mizpeh" — the two armies gathered for the coming war; the then the children of Ammon were gathered together, and encamped in Gilead (Ammon encamped); the and the children of Israel assembled themselves together, and encamped in Mizpeh (Israel encamped); the figure (the armies drawn up, the penitent people now facing the foe with hope of God's help; the truth that Israel, having repented, gathered to face Ammon, the deliverance near, Judges 11:29; the figure of the people who, having turned to God, now stand against the foe; the gathering of the armies; the muster for war, vv.16). The verse holds together the muster.

On "Then the children of Ammon were gathered together, and encamped in Gilead," the witnesses note Ammon. Gill notes Ammon encamping in Gilead (the muster, Judges 11:29) — Ammon (in Gilead).

On "And the children of Israel assembled themselves together, and encamped in Mizpeh," the grammatical-historical Ellicott and grammatical-historical note Israel: "Israel assembled themselves together, and encamped in Mizpeh" — Israel encamping at Mizpeh (the muster); the figure (the people who, having turned to God, stand against the foe); the providence (the muster, the search for a captain and Jephthah to follow, vv.18; ch.11). The verse displays Ammon and Israel gathering for war.

The very brief comment matches the very brief verse. The witnesses agree: the muster — "the children of Ammon were gathered together... 18:
"And the people and princes of Gilead said one to another, What man is he that will begin to fight against the children of Ammon? he shall be head over all the inhabitants of Gilead." The very brief verse notes the people of Gilead seeking a captain to lead against Ammon. The grammatical voices note the search. Keil and Delitzsch and Gill note that "the people and princes of Gilead said one to another, What man is he that will begin to fight against the children of Ammon? he shall be head over all the inhabitants of Gilead" — Gilead sought a leader, promising headship to whoever would fight Ammon; the and the people and princes of Gilead said one to another, What man is he that will begin to fight against the children of Ammon? (the search for a captain); the he shall be head over all the inhabitants of Gilead (the promise of headship); the figure (the people seeking a deliverer, the way prepared for Jephthah; the truth that Gilead's search for a captain prepared the way for Jephthah's rise, Judges 11:1; the figure of the providence that raises the deliverer through the people's need; the search for a captain; the way for Jephthah, vv.17). The verse closes the chapter, before the rise of Jephthah. The verse displays the people's need driving them to seek a leader, the providence preparing the deliverer God would raise up in Jephthah. The verse displays the truth that God works through the straits of his people to bring forth the saviour he has appointed.

The verse displays Gilead seeking a captain against Ammon.
